name: Touching Pleasure-Danger and Other “Binaries”
description: Join us this Pride Month for a lecture that asks what happens when we stop treating life as a choice between opposites.About the LecturePleasure or danger. Safety or adventure. Desire or shame.We are taught to navigate the world through binaries—to sort experiences into neat categories and choose the “right” side. But many of the things that make us feel most alive refuse such tidy distinctions.This lecture uses queer thought, feminism, and the lens of the psyche to explore the messy terrain between seeming opposites. Rather than treating contradiction as a problem to be solved, it asks what we might learn from embracing ambiguity, complexity, and the uncomfortable coexistence of conflicting feelings.Through stories, reflection, and conversation, the session explores:Why queerness is more than an identity category—it is also a way of seeing the worldHow pleasure and danger often intertwine in our emotional and erotic livesWhy safety and adventure are not always oppositesWhat our "yummy-yucky" desires can reveal about ourselvesHow binaries shape the way we understand sexuality, politics, and everyday lifeThis is not a lecture about becoming queer. It is a lecture about what queer ways of thinking can offer all of us: a richer language for desire, contradiction, and being human in uncertain times.Ultimately, the session invites us to look again at the desires, fears, and confusions we are often taught to suppress—and to ask whether they might contain important truths.About the SpeakerJaya Sharma is a feminist, queer, kinky activist and writer. As part of Nirantar, a feminist resource group, she worked on issues of gender and education for over two decades, while also leading sexuality trainings with organisations working alongside rural women.She has been deeply involved in queer activism in India, co-founding several queer forums in Delhi, including Voices Against 377. She is also a founding member of the Kinky Collective, a group dedicated to raising awareness about BDSM and strengthening the community through dialogue and education.Her current writing explores sexuality through the lens of the psyche, bringing together questions of desire, fantasy, intimacy, and selfhood.Ticket IncludesYour ticket includes a 250/- coupon redeemable on f&b at the venue.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen’s, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. Each event is an invitation to spend an evening with an idea.

name: The Invisible World Around Us
description: Join us for a lecture-demonstration that reveals the hidden systems quietly shaping everyday life, the signals, networks, and invisible conversations happening all around us, all the time.About the LectureModern life feels seamless.Your phone connects instantly. Devices speak to one another invisibly. Signals move through walls, across cities, and between bodies and machines without us ever seeing them.But beneath that apparent simplicity lies an invisible world of constant activity.This session is an interactive exploration of the hidden technological layer surrounding us every day, from wireless communication systems to biological signals generated within our own bodies.Through live demonstrations and visualisations, the lecture reveals the silent digital atmosphere we move through constantly, exposing how devices communicate, how networks remain connected, and how fragile these invisible systems can actually be.The session explores:The unseen wireless systems constantly operating around usHow digital communication networks function — and how easily they can be disruptedWhat invisible technological infrastructures modern life depends onHow biological signals inside the human body can be translated into machine movementWhat human-machine interaction might look like in the futureThe lecture also includes demonstrations involving EMG-controlled interfaces and experimental hardware systems that make invisible interactions tangible and visible in real time.About the SpeakerRahul Thareja is a hardware hacker and robotics engineer whose work explores the invisible systems shaping modern technological life.Working across experimental hardware, wireless system visualisation, and human-machine interfaces, his practice focuses on making hidden technological interactions visible, understandable, and open to questioning.With a background in robotics engineering and education, Rahul combines technical experimentation with a curiosity-driven approach to public engagement, creating interactive experiences that make complex systems tangible for broader audiences.His work sits at the intersection of exploration, awareness, and interaction — encouraging people to see familiar technologies in entirely unfamiliar ways.Your Ticket Includes a 300/- Coupon Redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Drafting Table
description: The Person Everyone Misunderstood - A Writing SessionOverviewSome people never got to explain themselves. They were judged in a moment, remembered by their worst, reduced to the part of the story that was easiest to tell. This month, we write them back into full shape. Bring a character, real, fictional, mythological, half-remembered, and write what the record missed.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to ExpectA Short Intro: We open with a brief framing of the theme and a prompt to get you started. No pressure to use it, just a way in.Writing Time: The bulk of the session is yours. Quiet, unhurried time to write whatever the character needs you to write.Open Mic: We close with an optional read-aloud. Share what you wrote, or just listen. Both are completely fine.Details🗓️ Date: 6th June (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Cafe Eos Dawn, M3M Urbana☕ Inclusion: Coffee included🎟️ Fee: ₹700

name: The House of Gonsalves (HOG) - Monsoon Table
description: Welcome to The House of Gonsalves (HOG) Supper Club 🌧️🥥We are Surya Gonsalves Yadav & Shreya Yadav — a mother-daughter duo hosting Delhi NCR's first Mangalorean supper club from our home in Gurgaon.Surya has been cooking Mangalorean food for over 40 years. Every masala ground by hand. Every coconut roasted slowly. Every curry paste built the way the coast taught her. Shreya grew up at this table learning every recipe, every technique, every story behind the dish. Now we cook together — opening our home and setting that table for you.This June marks our very first Monsoon Edition — inspired by the flavours of the Mangalorean coast during the rains. Fiery ghee roasts. Roasted coconut gravies. Kori roti. Seasonal mangoes. Dishes rooted in four decades of family recipes that haven't changed because they don't need to.This is not a traditional coursed meal. Dishes arrive slowly through the experience, the way they do in Mangalorean homes — meant to be shared, savoured and enjoyed over long conversations. You may arrive as strangers, but you’ll leave feeling like part of the table 💛Because this is our home, every seat matters to us. Limited seats. We close the table when it's full.Come to HOG at the HOG!With Love,Surya & Shreya

name: Chinese Whispers: India and China Before 1962
description: Join us for a lecture that revisits one of the most consequential relationships in modern Asian history, not through the lens of war, but through the fragile optimism that came before it.About the LectureToday, India and China are often discussed as rivals.But there was a moment when many believed the two newly independent civilizations would together shape the future of Asia and the postcolonial world.At the centre of that story stood K.M. Panikkar — historian, diplomat, strategist, and India’s ambassador to China during the tumultuous years following Mao’s revolution.What did Panikkar actually see when he arrived in Beijing after 1949? Why did he believe Communist China could be accommodated within an Asian framework rather than treated as an existential threat? And how did the promise of solidarity gradually give way to suspicion, miscalculation, and conflict?This lecture explores one of the most enduring debates in Indian foreign policy.The session examines:The early relationship between India and the newly established People's Republic of ChinaK.M. Panikkar’s role in shaping India's understanding of ChinaThe political and diplomatic tensions surrounding TibetHow the vision of Asian solidarity emerged — and why it ultimately fracturedWhether Panikkar misread China, or was unfairly blamed for a much larger strategic failureDrawing on archival research, diplomatic history, and political biography, the lecture revisits a period when the future of Asia appeared open, uncertain, and full of possibility.About the SpeakerNarayani Basu is a historian, foreign policy analyst, and award-winning author whose work focuses on the lesser-known individuals who shaped modern India.She is the bestselling author of V.P. Menon: The Unsung Architect of Modern India and Allegiance: Azaadi & the End of Empire, both widely praised for bringing overlooked figures and perspectives into the story of Indian independence.Her latest book, A Man for All Seasons, a biography of K.M. Panikkar, has received widespread critical acclaim and was awarded the AutHer Award for Best Non-Fiction Book in 2025.Through her writing and research, Narayani brings together biography, diplomacy, and political history to illuminate the personalities and decisions that continue to shape India's place in the world.Your Ticket Includes a 300/- Coupon Redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Good, the Bad, & the Ugly Sun
description: About the LectureThe Sun feels constant.Every morning it rises. It warms the planet. It makes life on Earth possible.But beneath that apparent stability lies a star in perpetual turbulence, a seething sphere of plasma and magnetic fields capable of eruptions so powerful they can disrupt satellites, collapse communication systems, and interfere with power grids across the planet.This lecture begins with one of the greatest mysteries in modern astrophysics: Why is the Sun’s outer atmosphere, the corona, hundreds of times hotter than its visible surface?It’s a paradox that seems to defy common sense, like walking away from a fire and somehow feeling warmer.From there, the session journeys into the hidden physics of the Sun: solar flares, coronal mass ejections, magnetic storms, and the invisible streams of charged particles constantly travelling toward Earth. The lecture revisits the famous Carrington Event of 1859 — the most intense solar storm ever recorded — when telegraph systems burst into sparks and auroras lit skies far beyond the poles.The session explores:Why the Sun is far more dynamic and unstable than it appearsHow solar eruptions shape “space weather” around EarthWhat happens when solar storms collide with modern technological systemsHow missions like India’s Aditya-L1 are helping scientists study and predict solar activityWhy understanding the Sun has become essential in a technology-dependent worldBlending astronomy, physics, history, and storytelling, this lecture invites us to see the Sun not as a distant backdrop to life on Earth — but as a powerful cosmic force we remain deeply dependent on, and vulnerable to.About the SpeakerProf. Vaibhav Pant is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics at IIT Delhi and a solar physicist whose work focuses on understanding the Sun and its impact on Earth.He completed his PhD from the Indian Institute of Astrophysics, Bangalore, followed by postdoctoral research at KU Leuven in Belgium and the Instituto de Astrofísica de Canarias in Spain. Before joining IIT Delhi, he served as Scientist at ARIES, Nainital for five years.His research explores waves in the solar atmosphere, large-scale solar eruptions, and space weather phenomena. He has published over 50 refereed research papers and has been closely associated with India’s Aditya-L1 solar mission, serving as Principal Investigator of the Aditya-L1 Support Cell and organizing multiple national workshops under the programme.Beyond research, he is deeply invested in science communication and has delivered invited talks across major international conferences in Asia and Europe. Outside physics, he enjoys reading non-fiction, playing table tennis, and watching thoughtful films and Korean dramas.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: Nizamuddin Basti Heritage walk on Sufism & Poetry
description: An immersive heritage walk through the lanes, stories, music, and living traditions of one of Delhi’s most evocative historic quarters (Nizamuddin Basti)Join Darwesh Heritage Walks for an evening exploring the layered world of Sufi thought, medieval Delhi, oral traditions, poetry, music, and the cultural landscape that evolved around the spiritual legacy of Hazrat Nizamuddin Auliya and Amir Khusrau.From forgotten Ghalib ki Mazaar and medieval structures to poetic memories carried through the basti’s winding lanes, this walk seeks to understand how love, devotion, mysticism, and art shaped the soul of the city.The walk includes storytelling, historical interpretation, and discussions around Sufism, poetry, architecture, and the intangible cultural heritage associated with the area.Disclaimer:  The tour route is in public areas over which we have no control/preferred access. description: Give your child two weeks away from screens and into a space filled with colour, curiosity, imagination, and hands-on making. ✨At Between Easels, children are introduced to 10 different art mediums through playful, guided sessions designed to help them explore freely, think creatively, and build confidence through art. From painting and printmaking to sculpture, collage, and clay — every class becomes a new creative adventure.Led by practicing artists in a warm and encouraging studio environment, the workshop focuses not on perfection, but on expression, observation, experimentation, and joy. 🎨The program concludes with a special exhibition where children proudly display their artworks for friends and family — celebrating everything they’ve created over the two weeks. 🖼️BETWEEN EASELS — KIDS WORKSHOP SCHEDULE10 Days • 10 Mediums • Endless Creativity⏰ Class Timing: 10 AM – 12 PM🎨 Suitable for all skill levels, ages 6+• 1 June (Mon) — Abstract ArtExplore colours, textures, movement, and playful mark-making through expressive painting exercises and creative experimentation.• 2 June (Tue) — Still Life DrawingLearn observation, light, shadows, and basic geometric forms through pencil and charcoal sketching.• 3 June (Wed) — CollagingCreate layered artworks using paper cut-outs, textures, found materials, and imaginative composition techniques.• 4 June (Thu) — Paint Like PicassoExperiment with bold colours, playful portraits, abstract faces, and cubist-inspired visual storytelling.• 5 June (Fri) — MonoprintingDiscover simple printmaking methods to create expressive, one-of-a-kind textured prints.✨ Weekend Off• 8 June (Mon) — Collective Painting & CollageWork together on large printmaking by layering materials to create embossed and experimental surfaces.• 12 June (Fri) — Clay PortraitsSculpt expressive clay portraits while learning facial structure, textures, and name: Delhi's Metro Morphosis: Metro Train heritage walk
description: Delhi’s Metro Morphosis-: A Walk Through Delhi’s Changing CommuteExploring the Impact of the Delhi Metro on Gender, Safety & Urban LifeIn the heart of India’s capital, the Delhi Metro is more than a transport system — it’s a living, moving map of the city’s transformation.Join us for a guided walkthrough and urban dialogue as we journey along the tracks — not just from station to station, but across the layers of Delhi’s social fabric. This unique walk explores how the Metro has quietly reshaped gender access, mobility justice, public spaces, urban design, and how heritage and history now sit side-by-side with modern infrastructure.We will cover Mandi House -Kailash Colony-Kashmere Gate-Chawari Bazar-Rajiv Chowk & Barakhamba Metro stations

name: Raat Ke Afsane : Qutub Minar
description: Experience the magic of Qutub Minar like never before—under the stars and glowing lights. This is a guided heritage walk led by a historian, who will take you through the stunning Qutub complex after dark. As the entire site is beautifully illuminated at night, the minaret shines like Delhi’s very own Eiffel Tower—majestic, mysterious, and unforgettable.Walk through centuries of history, discover forgotten stories, and explore Delhi’s medieval past through engaging storytelling. The play of light, shadow, and silence transforms this heritage site into something truly cinematicPlaces we will cover : 1. Mughal Serai2. Alai Minar3. Iltutmish Tomb4. Qutub Minar5. Iron Pilar6. 27 Jain and hindu temples7. Alai Darwaza8. Alauddin Khilji grave and madarsa .Important Points:  1. Details of the heritage walk will be mailed to you when you book the tickets2. No guided fees for kids below 5.3. Medium of communication : Hindi and English4. Please note that in case of unavoidable external reasons such as (weather conditions, political rallies, VIP movement, powercut) the walk may be canceled. The participant has the liberty to take another walk by the company against the payment they have made within 30 days since the date of the registered walk.5. The company does not have refund policy.

location.address: Qutub Minar Complex Rd, Seth Sarai, Mehrauli, New Delhi, Delhi 110030, India

name: Tracing the Djinns of Kotla Ferozshah
description: TRACING THE DJINNS of FEROZSHAH KOTLA FORTAcross the old walls, we will gather at the main gate of Feroz Shah Kotla. Before us lie crumbling stone portals, hollowed arches, and silent courtyards that once bustled with life in the 14th century under Sultan Feroz Shah Tughlaq.This is no ordinary heritage walk. We will tread the boundary between history and myth — stepping into the realm of the djinns said to haunt these ruins, to listen, to judge, and sometimes to grant wishes.Led by expert storyteller and myth-hunter, the walk will weave together folklore, historical lore, and spooky tales . Expect atmospheric narration, dramatic pauses, unexpected twists — and maybe a few ghostly encounters.Not for the faint hearted !!Highlights* A guided heritage walk through ruins, hidden chambers, and alleyways* Folklore and legends of djinns haunting the fort* Dramatic storytelling * Occasional “mystery stops” and whispers* A themed ritual (writing wishes / invoking legends) in the end.

name: Open SketchBook
description: Open SketchBook: You don’t need to leave the city to feel grounded.OverviewOur core belief is that you do not need to leave the city to feel grounded. In a world where everyone is distracted, we pause as a group to give our attention to one shared thing. Sketching becomes the medium to notice small, living details in urban spaces that we usually rush through.You do not need to be an artist to enjoy sketching. Open SketchBook is a session for anyone who wants to explore drawing as a way to relax and observe the world. It is about the process of putting pencil to paper rather than the final masterpiece.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to expectGuided Observation: Learn simple ways to look at objects or spaces and translate them into lines and shapes.Creative Freedom: A judgment free zone where you can experiment with sketching without worrying about "doing it right."Tactile Experience: Use your new diary and pencil kit to capture what you see in the moment.Relaxed Environment: Enjoy a late afternoon at Greenr with a small group of people who just want to create and connect.Details🗓️ Date: 23rd May (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:00 AM📍 Location: Greenr, Golf Course Ext Road🎟️ Contribution: ₹700 (Includes an A6 diary and pencil kit)

location.address: Ground Floor, AIPL Joy Central, GF-01, Golf Course Ext Rd, Sector 65, Gurugram, Haryana 122002, India

name: The Therapeutic Art of  Wood Whittling
description: Step into the calming world of carving with The Therapeutic Art of Wood Whittling — a first-of-its-kind hands-on whittling experience in Delhi NCR, hosted at our studio by the incredibly talented artist Viney Anand, who brings over 5 years of wood whittling experience to the craft.Designed for beginners and creative enthusiasts alike, this immersive workshop is all about slowing down, working with your hands, and experiencing the quiet satisfaction of transforming a simple piece of wood into something meaningful. Under Viney’s guidance, you’ll learn the fundamentals of safe wood whittling, carving techniques, tool handling, and finishing methods in a warm and welcoming environment.All tools, safety equipment, and materials are fully included, so you can simply arrive and enjoy the process. By the end of the session, you’ll take home your very own handcrafted wooden project along with a deeply therapeutic and memorable experience.Whether you’re looking to unwind, explore a new skill, reconnect with creativity, or simply experience the joy of slow craftsmanship, this is a workshop you wouldn’t want to miss.

name: Reminiscing Sa'di: A Legacy of Persian Literature
description: Join us for a lecture that traces one of the most influential cultural exchanges in South Asian history — a centuries-long relationship between India and Persia that shaped the languages we speak, the literature we read, and the ways power itself was imagined.About the LectureFor centuries, Persian was not a foreign language in India.It was the language of courts and administration, poetry and philosophy, diplomacy and social mobility. Long before English occupied that role, Persian connected vast parts of the subcontinent to a broader Persianate world stretching from Anatolia to Central Asia.Its influence was so deep that generations of Indian scholars, poets, bureaucrats, and intellectuals began their education through Persian. Communities such as Brahmins, Kayasthas, and Khatris built careers through their mastery of the language, while poets from Iran travelled to India seeking patronage, literary fame, and intellectual companionship.This lecture explores the remarkable journey of Persian in India — not simply as an imported language, but as a tradition transformed by the people who adopted it.The session explores:Why Persian became the language of power and culture in HindustanThe lives and journeys of Persian poets who made India their homeHow Indian scholars and writers reshaped Persian literary traditionsThe ethical and political worlds contained in texts by Sa'di, Hafiz, Rumi, and othersThe influence of Persian on Hindi, Urdu, and other Indian vernacularsHow Persian civilisation retained its distinct identity across centuries of political and religious changeMoving between history, literature, politics, and storytelling, the lecture reveals a world in which ideas, languages, and people moved across borders far more freely than we often imagine today.At its heart, this is a story about exchange: how cultures borrow, adapt, and reinvent one another — and how those encounters continue to echo in our everyday lives.About the SpeakersDr. Mukhtar Ahmed teaches at the Department of Persian, University of Delhi, where his research focuses on Modern Persian Literature and Indo-Persian literary traditions. His forthcoming book, From Persia to Paris: A Journey of Ideas and Cultures, examines the intellectual and literary exchanges between Persian and French traditions. Known among friends for both his scholarship and his cooking, he remains deeply interested in the movement of ideas across languages and civilisations.Heena Goswami is a public policy professional with an academic background in history and has recently submitted her PhD dissertation at the University of Delhi. Born and raised in Rajasthan, her interests span history, politics, technology, and public life. An avid reader and former educator, she now spends much of her time encouraging others to read, often through stories about books and ideas shared online.Your ticket includes a 300/- coupon redeemable on F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: ALT EFF Film Club x GuruJal Gurugram | 19th June
description: Join us for a screening of Miles Away; a visual and auditory immersion of the lives of migrant, female brick labors. This film is intimate, cinematic and sensory.Part of our 2024 Program, this was Juror Dia Mirza's reaction:"I was very moved by 'Miles Away' as for the first time I saw the perspective of what Climate Change is doing to women. It was very special to see so many women coming together to tell the story and feeling at ease as they shared personal moments with the female filmmakers. I felt as if I was a part of their lives."To become a Film Club Member, continue with the regular booking process. The membership option will appear after you select the venue and date.All activities, including discussions and refreshments, will occur between 7:00 and 9:00 PM.What's Included?Exclusive screenings of the world's best environmental cinemaAccess to a community of environmentally inclined, film-loving peoplePost-film discussions.Student Discount50% off for students with a valid ID (& must bring ID to the screening).CODE: ALTSTUDENTABOUT ALT EFFIf you are tired of the outrage cycle, doomscrolling collapse, and the looming AI takeover narrative. If you have caught yourself going numb just to cope. We see you.All Living Things Environmental Film Festival is back with the third season of Film Club. A monthly meet up across 12 cities over 5 months, orbiting environmental cinema that cracks you open without trapping you in doom and gloom.It is a room full of people choosing to show up in cities whose infrastructure persuades you to stay home, choosing to sit together, watch together, and turn private anxiety into collective imagination. Messy. Human. Honest conversation. With snacks. Always snacks.

location.address: 6/9, F-2, Block F, DLF Phase 1, Sector 26A, Gurugram, Haryana 122002, India

name: The Making Of A Serial Offender
description: Join us for an evening that begins with a simple question: what makes a serial offender? The answer may be more unsettling than you think.About the LectureThis lecture explores how criminal profiling moved beyond outdated assumptions about "criminal faces" to become one of the most fascinating tools in modern investigations. Drawing from forensic psychology, criminology, and real-world case studies, the session examines how investigators attempt to understand offenders through behaviour rather than appearance.Together, we'll explore:Why humans are often poor judges of who is capable of violenceThe evolution of criminal profiling, from early theories to modern investigative psychologyHow crime scene behaviour can reveal patterns of motive, planning, control, and escalationThe origins of behavioural profiling and the rise of the FBI's Behavioural Science UnitWhat famous serial offender cases reveal about fantasy, victim selection, and criminal developmentWhy serial offending remains understudied in IndiaHow forensic psychologists and investigators use Behavioural Evidence Analysis to reconstruct offender behaviourMoving beyond sensationalism and popular crime shows, this lecture examines the deeper psychological and social questions that profiling raises.Why do some individuals cross lines that most never do? What can behaviour tell us about risk, intent, and future offending? And how can India build more rigorous behavioural science frameworks for criminal investigation?Part psychology, part criminology, and part investigative science, this session offers a rare glimpse into the complex process of understanding criminal behaviour.About the SpeakerMr. Rithin Joseph is an Assistant Professor at the School of Behavioural Forensics, National Forensic Sciences University (NFSU), Delhi.Specialising in Criminology and Forensic Psychology, his work focuses on criminal profiling, deception detection, investigative psychology, and forensic behavioural analysis. He has published multiple research papers and presented over twenty-one papers at national and international conferences.Mr. Joseph has specialised training in a range of forensic investigative techniques, including Polygraph, Brain Electrical Oscillation Signature Profiling (BEOS), Suspect Detection System (SDS), Layered Voice Analysis (LVA), and EyeDetect.He currently serves as Co-Investigator on an ICSSR-funded research project examining the prediction of recidivism among first-time offenders in conflict with the law. He is also the co-author of *Crime as the Cause of Criminality* and *BRICS: Unravelling the Abyss*.A Gold Medalist in Criminology from the National Forensic Sciences University and recipient of the **Young Criminologist Award 2024**, he continues to contribute to research in criminal psychology, offender behaviour, and investigative science while mentoring postgraduate students and pursuing doctoral research in Criminal Psychology.Your ticket includes an alcoholic/non-alcoholic beverage of your choice!About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

Blinkit bags, newspapers, old book pages, packaging paper. If it folds, it works. Come make something with it.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. name: Daughters of Shahjahan at Red fort (A Heritage walk)
description: Daughters of Shahjahan Heritage walk at the Redfort (With Dramatic Reading of play excerpts)Join Darwesh Heritage Walks & Sabhyata Foundation for a unique Immersive experience at the Red Fort .The Red Fort (Lal Qila), an iconic symbol of India's history, stands proudly in Old Delhi as a silent witness to centuries of political, architectural, and cultural change. Commissioned by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in 1638, this UNESCO World Heritage Site is not just a monument—it is a storyteller, narrating tales of imperial grandeur, colonial resistance, and post-independence pride. Stories about the Mughal opulence, the power politics, the powerful place of women in our history will unfold as we walk through the bygone Mughal era.The ‘war of succession’ that took place among the four sons of Shah Jahan had one novelty, while previously, wars were fought after the death of the emperor, this war was fought when Shah Jahan was still alive. All sons and daughters of Shah Jahan participated in it. While the four sons Dara Shikoh, Shah Shuja, Aurangzeb and Murad Baksh fought against each other, the sisters Jahanara and Roshanara allied themselves with one or the other brother. Through this we will bring to you to the story of the Mughal battle of succession that changed the course of Indian history.The heritage walk includes (dramatic reading of play excerpts in Hindustani) highlighting stories and scenes from the tussle going on between Shahjahan’s Sons Aurangzeb and Dara Shikoh and the two daughters Jahanara and Roshanara Begum, each of whom sided with one brother.

location.address: Netaji Subhash Marg, Lal Qila, Chandni Chowk, New Delhi, Delhi, 110006, India

name: Before You Blame
description: Before You Blame: A Workshop on Navigating Relationships with ClarityOverviewMost relationship conflicts are not just about what happened; they are about how we interpret what happened. This session focuses on exploring how our thoughts shape our connections and why friction often feels more intense than the situation warrants. "Before You Blame" is a reflective workshop designed to help you look at your assumptions and inner patterns, moving you from reactivity toward steadiness and emotional responsibility.What is Stories of Hope?We are a community-led initiative dedicated to creating safe and mindful spaces. Through intimate workshops and gatherings, we help individuals navigate life’s complexities, from mental well-being to personal growth, with empathy and shared human connection.What to ExpectPattern Recognition: Explore how common emotional triggers and ego-driven patterns shape conflicts in marriage, family, or work.Internal Focus: Instead of trying to fix others, we gently shift the focus inward to understand our own interpretations and responses.Practical Tools: Learn actionable ways to question stressful thoughts and practice perspective shifts using real-life scenarios.Safe Exploration: A supportive, non-judgmental environment for honest discussion. This is a space for learning and reflection, not a lecture or therapy.Key TakeawaysClarity: Gain a deeper understanding of recurring relationship dynamics.Responsiveness: Walk away with tools to help you respond thoughtfully instead of reacting impulsively.Emotional Ownership: Build the internal steadiness needed to navigate life without requiring others to change first.Details🗓️ Date: 21st June (Sunday)⏰ Time: 11:00 AM📍 Location: Kanchanjunga Towers, Sector 56

location.address: A-BLOCK, KANCHANJUNGA APARTMENT, G.H.18, near Post Office, Sector 56, Gurugram, Haryana 122011, India

name: Sex, Death, And The Long War Of Life
description: Join us for a lecture that begins with a provocative proposition:You were never meant to exist.And that may be the most liberating fact about you.About the LectureHuman beings like stories.We tell ourselves that life has meaning, that history bends toward progress, that our desires are uniquely our own.Evolution tells a different story.One without heroes, villains, purpose, or justice.This lecture explores the oldest conflict on Earth: the endless struggle of living things to survive, reproduce, and persist. Long before nations, religions, or political ideologies existed, life was already engaged in a ruthless competition that continues to shape every organism alive today.Drawing on evolutionary biology and psychology, the session traces how some of our deepest instincts emerged from this ancient inheritance.The lecture explores:Why evolution remains the most powerful framework for understanding lifeHow competition and cooperation coexist in the natural worldWhat sex is really doing from the perspective of evolutionWhy human beings are, like all organisms, unfinished creaturesHow morality, consciousness, and even the idea of a “soul” might be viewed through an evolutionary lensMoving from algae to human behaviour, from reproduction to mortality, the session challenges many of the comforting assumptions we hold about ourselves.At its heart lies a difficult but strangely freeing question:What changes when we stop imagining that life was designed for us?About the SpeakerAmbarish Satwik is a Delhi-based vascular and endovascular surgeon, Director of the Vascular Cath Lab at Sir Ganga Ram Hospital, and Professor of Vascular Surgery at GRIPMER.Alongside his medical career, he is an acclaimed writer whose work moves across literature, history, politics, and science. His novel Perineum: Nether Parts of the Empire is a critically acclaimed exploration of sexuality and power in the British Raj.His essays, columns, and commentaries have appeared in publications including India Today, The Times of India, Outlook, Open, Tehelka, Time Out, and Hindu Business Line.Known for bringing together scientific rigour, intellectual irreverence, and dark humour, his talks often leave audiences questioning ideas they previously took for granted.Ticket IncludesYour ticket includes a complimentary drink during the session.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Procrastireaders
description: The Procrastireaders: Love is Love - Pride Month ReadingOverviewThis June, Procrastireaders is reading stories about queer love. The joy, the tenderness, the ordinary and extraordinary ways people find each other. Stories where being queer is not the conflict to be resolved but simply part of who the characters are. If that sounds like your kind of read, come join us.What is Stories of Hope?Stories of Hope is a community built around a simple idea, that people need places where they can slow down and be heard. We create small, intentional spaces for writing, listening, creating, and reflecting. There is no expectation to perform and no pressure to have the right words. Just a space to pause and be with what is already there.What to ExpectDiscussion First: We open with a conversation around the theme, the books you brought, and what drew you to them. No right answers, just an honest exchange.Silent Reading Block: We close with dedicated quiet time to actually sit with your book. Good lighting, AC, and fellow readers for company.Bring Your Own Book: Carry whatever you are currently reading or something from your "to be read" pile that needs your attention.Details🗓️ Date: 27th June (Saturday)⏰ Time: 11:30 AM📍 Location: Unlocked, 32nd Avenue🎟️ Fee: ₹700

name: 4D Polymers: 3 Dimensions & Beyond
description: Join us for a lecture that explores a future where materials don't simply exist, they respond, transform, heal, and even generate their own energy.About the LectureMost materials are passive.A bandage covers a wound. An implant supports damaged tissue. A scaffold holds cells in place.But what if materials could do more?What if they could change shape over time, respond to their environment, generate electrical signals, and actively participate in the healing process?This lecture introduces the emerging world of 4D biomaterials — materials designed not only in three dimensions, but with a fourth dimension: time.Drawing from cutting-edge research in regenerative medicine and bioengineering, the session explores how scientists are creating materials that can mimic some of the remarkable properties of living tissue. These next-generation biomaterials are capable of programmed transformations, self-powered stimulation, and dynamic interactions with the cells around them.The lecture explores:What 4D printing is and how it differs from conventional 3D printingHow materials can be designed to change shape and function over timeThe role of bioelectric signals in tissue healing and regenerationHow self-powered biomaterials can generate electrical stimulation without batteries or external energy sourcesWhy these technologies could transform the future of healthcare, implants, and regenerative medicineAlong the way, the session offers a glimpse into a future where materials are no longer passive tools but active participants in biological processes.About the SpeakerDr. Amit Nain is an Assistant Professor at the Kusuma School of Biological Sciences, IIT Delhi, where he leads research focused on developing next-generation biomaterials for tissue regeneration and advanced healthcare applications.His laboratory works at the intersection of materials science, biology, and engineering, designing bioinstructive materials that can sense, respond, and support healing processes within the body.Dr. Nain completed his B.Tech. in Biotechnology from VIT University and earned his PhD in Nano Science and Technology from National Taiwan University, where his research focused on antimicrobial nanomaterials.His work has been recognised internationally, including being named a 2024 Emerging Investigator by the Royal Society of Chemistry and a 2025 Rising Star by the American Chemical Society. He has published over 35 research articles, book chapters, and conference proceedings, and currently serves on the Early Career Board of Biomacromolecules.Outside the lab, he is a Bollywood enthusiast, poetry lover, road-trip addict, and an unapologetic fan of Friends, Breaking Bad, Homeland, and Money Heist.Your ticket includes a 200/- voucher for F&B About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: From mehfils to movies: Courtesans of India
description: A Heritage Baithak at Qudsia BaghWhat happens when history remembers a woman only through the lens of morality and forgets her art, intellect, and influence?Join us for a fascinating journey into the world of India's courtesans—Nagarvadhus, Devadasis, Kanchanis, Tawaifs, and Nautch Girls—women who were once among the most accomplished artists, poets, musicians, patrons, and cultural icons of their age. Many enjoyed wealth, education, and social influence, supported temples and religious institutions, shaped artistic traditions, and moved comfortably within royal courts.Yet, with changing political and colonial attitudes, these women found themselves increasingly stigmatized, their identities reduced to stereotypes and their contributions pushed to the margins of history. We will trace this remarkable transformation and explore how many eventually carried their artistic legacy into the emerging world of Indian cinema, becoming singers, actors, dancers, and pioneers of the film industry.Through stories of power, performance, patronage, and prejudice, this walk seeks to rediscover the women who helped shape India's cultural landscape but whose voices have often been forgotten.Join Darwesh Heritage Walks at Qudsia Bagh as we uncover the extraordinary lives of India's courtesans—the custodians of art, culture, and memory.

location.address: 118, Yamuna Marg, Prema Kunj, Civil Lines, New Delhi, Delhi, 110054, India

name: Among Behemoths: On Tigers, Territory, And People
description: Join us for a lecture that challenges one of the most enduring myths of the natural world:That tigers belong somewhere far away from us.About the LectureWhen we think of tigers, we often imagine a solitary predator lurking deep inside untouched forests — a powerful but distant creature whose world exists entirely apart from our own.Much of modern conservation has been built around this image.But the reality is far more complicated.Across India, tigers move through landscapes shaped not only by forests, but by roads, farms, villages, canals, and human lives. Their stories are intertwined with ours in ways that are often overlooked, revealing a world where conflict and coexistence exist side by side.Drawing on years of fieldwork and a lifetime spent exploring India's forests, this lecture looks beyond the mythology of the tiger to understand the animal itself — its behaviour, its adaptability, and its relationship with the people who share its territory.The session explores:Why many popular assumptions about tigers are misleadingHow tiger behaviour differs from the image of the relentless apex predatorThe changing meaning of "wild" in an increasingly human-shaped worldHow communities and tigers negotiate shared landscapesWhat conservation looks like beyond the idea of keeping nature separate from peopleMoving between ecology, anthropology, conservation, and storytelling, the lecture examines what happens when we stop thinking of wilderness as a place apart and begin seeing it as something deeply entangled with human life.At its core, this is a story about adaptation — not only of tigers, but of all of us.About the SpeakerRudraksha Rishi Mitra is a social innovator, researcher, and founder of Canopy Wild, an immersive wildlife travel and education company dedicated to deepening public engagement with conservation and the natural world.A graduate of the London School of Economics in International Social and Public Policy, he has worked extensively across public policy, sustainable development, and social entrepreneurship. In 2022, he was selected as an Unleash Global Talent, recognising him among the world's leading young social innovators.His research has focused on conservation policy and human–tiger interactions in the Terai Arc Landscape, combining ethnographic methods with ecological inquiry. Over the years, he has explored and documented tiger landscapes across India, including the Nilgiri Biosphere, Annamalais, Terai Arc, and the Dooars.Having spent much of his life among forests and wildlife habitats, he is particularly interested in the ways people and large carnivores continue to share space in a rapidly changing world.Ticket IncludesYour ticket includes a 500/- coupon for F&BAbout unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.
